name: SL Thomas screenshot exam seed question bank
description: Questions extracted from Sc_lang_thomas.pdf and corrected using Python semantics / lecture content.
coverage_note: The PDF contains 17 screenshots from a 20-question ePortal quiz. It includes quiz numbers 1-10, 12-16, 18, and 20; quiz numbers 11, 17, and 19 were not visible in the PDF.

questions.question: You have defined a function: def myfunction(x=5, y=7). All following calls are correct: myfunction(3), myfunction(y=8), myfunction().
questions.explanation: Both parameters have default values; one positional argument, one keyword argument, or no arguments are all valid.

questions.question: When you run code to iterate over a tuple: for a in ('abc', 'def', 'ghi'): print(a), you will see an error.
questions.explanation: Tuples are iterable. The loop prints abc, def, ghi on separate lines.

questions.question: You have defined a function: def f(a, b): # body. If you call f(6), variable a will be equal to 6 and variable b will be equal to 0.
questions.explanation: b has no default value. Calling f(6) raises TypeError for the missing required argument b.

questions.question: You can add a new element to the tuple using append(element) method.
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable and do not have append(). Lists have append().

questions.question: If you set logging level to WARNING, all DEBUG messages will also be displayed.
questions.explanation: WARNING shows WARNING and more severe messages, such as ERROR and CRITICAL. DEBUG is lower priority and is hidden.

questions.question: When you run: for a in ['abcd']: print(a). In the first printed line you will see: abcd.
questions.explanation: The list has one element: the string 'abcd'. The first and only printed value is abcd.

questions.question: If you perform append([4, 5, 6]) on the list [1, 2, 3], its length will be 4.
questions.explanation: append() adds its argument as one element. The list becomes [1, 2, 3, [4, 5, 6]], length 4.

questions.question: You have a list l = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. The sum of elements defined by slice [1:3] equals 5.
You can add a new element to the list using append(element) method.
questions.explanation: append(x) adds x at the end of a list.

questions.question: The command assert c==4 raises an exception if c is not equal 4.
questions.explanation: In normal execution, a failed assert raises AssertionError. With Python -O, asserts can be ignored.

questions.question: If you perform extend([4, 5, 6]) on the list [1, 2, 3], its length will be 4.
questions.explanation: extend() adds each element from the iterable. The list becomes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], length 6.

questions.question: age = 20; s = 'Jane is ' + (age+5) + ' years old'; print(s). This code will display: Jane is 25 years old.
questions.explanation: The expression tries to concatenate a string with an int. Python raises TypeError unless the number is converted to str or formatted.

questions.question: When you run: for a in 'abcd': print(a). In the first printed line you will see: abcd.
questions.explanation: Iterating over a string gives one character at a time. The first printed value is 'a'.

questions.question: The length of the tuple created with tuple('123') is 3.
questions.explanation: tuple('123') creates ('1', '2', '3'), so the length is 3.

questions.question: If you run the for loop with range(15, 0, -5), the last value in the range is 5.
questions.explanation: The generated values are 15, 10, 5. The stop value 0 is excluded.

questions.topic: Sorting tuples with lambda / sorted() return value
questions.question: You have comps = (("Lenovo", 2499), ("Toshiba", 1699), ("HP", 2099)). If you sort the tuple using sorted(comps, key=lambda laptop: laptop[1]), the first element of the tuple comps is ("Toshiba", 1699).
questions.explanation: sorted(...) returns a new sorted list and does not modify the original tuple comps. The sorted result starts with ('Toshiba', 1699), but comps itself still starts with ('Lenovo', 2499).
questions.note: Ambiguous wording: if the exam means 'first element of the sorted result', the expected answer could be True. For strict Python semantics, False.

questions.question: You have opened file with f=open('file.txt'). The command f.read() reads a single line from the file f.
questions.explanation: f.read() reads the whole file content. f.readline() reads one line.
